title: Map technique suggestions back into assurance case as evidence
description: Take the recommended techniques from Step 3 and map them back into the assurance case JSON as new evidence nodes. Evaluate how well the MCP-driven workflow closes the loop between gap identification and evidence generation. May revisit approach based on learnings from Steps 2-3.

title: Enhancement skills should evaluate existing relationships, not just add new ones
description:
## Issue

During rlw.7 integration testing, we found that the enhancement specialist skills (academic, practitioner, standards) only propose **additions** to `related_techniques`. They don't evaluate whether **existing** relationships are relevant.

## Example

For `internal-review-boards`, the existing related techniques include:
- `red-teaming` - adversarial testing (weak relationship to governance)
- `safety-envelope-testing` - technical boundary testing (weak relationship)

These weren't flagged as potentially irrelevant, while new relationships were proposed.

## Proposed Changes

Update the specialist skills in `.claude/skills/enhancement/` to:

1. **Evaluate existing relationships** for relevance/strength
2. **Propose removals** when relationships are weak or tangential
3. **Assess relationship type** (methodological, complementary, comparative)

## Files to Modify

- `.claude/skills/enhancement/academic-perspective/SKILL.md`
- `.claude/skills/enhancement/practitioner-perspective/SKILL.md`
- `.claude/skills/enhancement/standards-perspective/SKILL.md`

## Discovered

During rlw.7 integration testing of the enhancement system.

title: Add Bun support to MCP server quick start
description: Update the MCP server quick start documentation to support Bun as well as Node.js. Also clarify that the MCP server now works with both local and remote data access modes.

title: MCP Server evaluation findings: search and filtering gaps
description:
## Summary

Evaluation of the TEA Techniques MCP server as a copilot for assurance case gap-filling, using a cardiac digital twin (lumped-parameter physics model) for PAH assessment. Tested 5 property claims with no evidence against a pre-established rubric.

## Critical Bug

**suggest_techniques_for_claim returns nothing for any input.**

Root cause: `mcp-server/src/graph/index.ts:162` — `findTechniques()` uses `terms.every(term => text.includes(term))`, an AND across all whitespace-split query terms. Multi-word queries (i.e. any natural language claim) almost never match because every word must appear in a single technique's name+description. The `suggest_techniques_for_claim` tool delegates to `findTechniques` with the full claim text as the query, so it is completely non-functional.

## Structural Gaps

### 1. No model-type filtering
There is no way to exclude NN-specific techniques when working with non-neural-network models (e.g. physics-based, statistical, simulation). Tags like `model-agnostic` exist but NN-specific techniques still dominate results. OOD Detector for Neural Networks, Deep Ensembles, Monte Carlo Dropout all surface for a lumped-parameter physics model.

### 2. Keyword ambiguity across ML subfields
Domain terms have different meanings in different contexts:
- "sensitivity" → returns Prompt Sensitivity Analysis (LLM) instead of parameter sensitivity analysis
- "calibration" → returns Calibration with Equality of Opportunity (fairness) alongside Empirical Calibration (reliability)
- "interpretable" → returns LIME, ANCHOR, GAMs (black-box ML explainability) instead of process/stakeholder techniques

### 3. Process techniques buried by ML techniques
For claims about clinical interpretability (P4.1), the 3 relevant process techniques (Human-in-the-Loop, Model Cards, Automated Documentation) were overwhelmed by 6 irrelevant ML explainability techniques (LIME, GAMs, RuleFit, ANCHOR, Occlusion Sensitivity, Concept Activation Vectors).

## Scoring Results

| Claim | Good (+1) | Acceptable (0) | Bad (-1) | Verdict |
|-------|-----------|----------------|----------|---------|
| P1.2 Fidelity | 1 | 1 | 3 | Poor |
| P2.2 Calibration convergence | 3 | 1 | 3 | Mixed |
| P3.2 OOD/reliability flagging | 3 | 2 | 2 | Mixed |
| P4.1 Interpretability for clinicians | 3 | 2 | 6 | Poor |
| P4.2 Clinical endpoint validation | 3 | 0 | 0 | Good (but required careful keyword selection) |

## Evaluation Context
- Assurance case: Cardiac Digital Twin for PAH Assessment (imported into TEA Platform)
- Case ID: fe0017fe-201e-4515-8f4c-1dadc03b8582
- Model type: lumped-parameter (0D) physics model (NOT a neural network)
- Rubric: +1 good (directly relevant, model-appropriate), 0 acceptable (tangentially useful), -1 bad (wrong model type/domain/keyword match)
- Key constraint: NN-specific techniques scored -1 because the system under assurance is a physics model

## Files
- Assurance case JSON: `AssurancePlatform/TASKS/test-cases/Cardiac-DT-PAH-2026-02-13T12-30-00.json`
- MCP server search logic: `mcp-server/src/graph/index.ts:130-177`
- MCP server claim suggestion: `mcp-server/src/server.ts:250+`
close_reason: Implemented Fuse.js fuzzy search, excludeTags filter, concept-tag claim mapping, and excludeModelTypes parameter

title: Add UI for viewing depth-3+ assurance goal category tags
description:
## Issue

The website currently has no way to view or navigate secondary/tertiary assurance goal category tags (depth-3+).

When we add tags like:
- `assurance-goal-category/fairness/procedural`
- `assurance-goal-category/transparency/accountability`
- `assurance-goal-category/safety/human-oversight`

These create new taxonomy nodes, but users cannot:
1. Browse techniques by these subcategories
2. See the full taxonomy hierarchy on filter pages
3. Understand the relationship between depth-2 and depth-3 tags

## Current State

- Filter pages exist for depth-1 categories (e.g., /filters/assurance-goal-category/)
- Some depth-2 filters exist but navigation is limited
- Depth-3+ tags are applied to techniques but not exposed in UI

## Proposed Solution

1. Add hierarchical navigation to category filter pages
2. Show subcategory breakdown on goal pages
3. Consider tree view or nested accordion for taxonomy exploration

## Discovered

During rlw.7 integration testing when adding depth-3 assurance-goal-category tags.

title: Identify evidence gaps in the assurance case
description: Review the fleshed-out assurance case and identify property claims that lack sufficient evidence. This will be more complex once the case has proper strategies and decomposed sub-arguments. The gaps will serve as inputs for the TEA Techniques MCP agent in Step 3.
close_reason: 5 evidence gaps identified (P1.2, P2.2, P3.2, P4.1, P4.2) with evaluation rubric established: +1 good, 0 acceptable, -1 bad. Key constraint: physics model not NN, so NN-specific techniques are bad results.

title: Add periodic tag taxonomy consolidation report
description:
## Problem

The enhancement system adds new tags to techniques over time, which is expected and good. However, this creates a long-tail distribution problem where many tags apply to only 1-2 techniques. The existing taxonomy already exhibits this:

- **assurance-goal-category**: ~15 tags with only 1 technique (counterfactual, model card, pruning, etc.)
- **applicable-models**: ~10 singleton tags (feedforward, regression, gam, logistic, etc.)
- **lifecycle-stage**: near-duplicates like `deployment` vs `system deployment and use`, `monitoring` vs `system use and monitoring`
- **data-requirements**: 5 singleton tags

As enhancement agents continue adding tags, this will worsen — hurting discoverability and making the taxonomy unwieldy for users.

## Solution

Create a CLI report tool (`pnpm tag-audit`) that flags taxonomy health issues for human review. The report should identify:

### Detection criteria
1. **Singleton/sparse tags**: Tags with fewer than N techniques (configurable threshold, default 3)
2. **Near-duplicate tags**: Tags with high string similarity (edit distance) or semantic overlap within the same category
3. **High-overlap tags**: Tag pairs where technique membership has high Jaccard similarity (i.e. they're applied to nearly the same set of techniques, suggesting redundancy)
4. **Orphan deep tags**: Depth-3+ tags with no siblings in the hierarchy
5. **Category imbalance**: Categories where >30% of tags are singletons

### Output
- Structured report (terminal output + optional JSON/markdown)
- Grouped by category
- Each flagged tag shows: technique count, similar tags, suggested action (merge candidate, review, keep)

### Scope
- Covers the **entire taxonomy**, not just enhancement-generated tags
- The original hand-curated taxonomy already has long-tail issues that should be addressed

### Process
- Run periodically (suggested: every 6 months, or after batch enhancement runs)
- Human reviews report and makes merge/keep/remove decisions
- Changes applied manually to techniques.json (or via a future helper script)

## Non-goals
- This issue does NOT include an automated merge tool — just the diagnostic report
- This issue does NOT change the enhancement agent behavior (that's covered by tea-techniques-xkx)

## Technical notes
- Script location: `scripts/tag-audit.ts` (or similar)
- Data source: `public/data/techniques.json`
- Could reuse taxonomy exploration from the MCP server for consistency

title: Investigate cross-cutting/general-purpose techniques architecture
description:
## Problem

Some techniques (e.g., internal-review-boards) are cross-cutting and could support ANY assurance goal, not just one specific goal. Current dataset architecture requires techniques to be tagged with specific assurance goals.

## Specific Case

During enhancement analysis of `internal-review-boards` (score 67), agents proposed adding Privacy as an assurance goal because IRBs address privacy implications. However, IRBs also address fairness, safety, reliability, transparency, etc.

## Questions to Investigate

1. Should we introduce a "General" or "All" assurance goal category?
2. Should cross-cutting techniques be tagged with ALL applicable goals?
3. Should we have a separate field/flag to indicate "applies to all goals"?
4. How should filtering/search handle general-purpose techniques?
5. What are examples of other cross-cutting techniques in the dataset?

## Impact

- Dataset architecture (assurance_goals field semantics)
- Taxonomy structure (potential new category)
- Filtering/search behavior (should general techniques always appear?)
- Data generation logic
- UI/UX for displaying cross-cutting techniques

## Next Steps

1. Audit dataset for other cross-cutting techniques
2. Research how similar datasets handle this
3. Design proposed solution with trade-offs
4. Update enhancement workflow to handle edge cases
5. Document decision in dataset documentation

title: TEA Techniques Knowledge Graph & MCP Server
description: Formalise the TEA techniques dataset as a knowledge graph with a lightweight ontology (JSON-LD), expose it via an MCP server for LLM-powered technique discovery, and serve enriched data as static endpoints. Progressive approach: vocabulary → JSON-LD enrichment → MCP server → future graph infrastructure as needed. Three target consumers: (1) LLM agents helping users find techniques for natural language queries, (2) TEA Assurance Platform integration for structured claim→evidence→technique queries, (3) researchers studying patterns in Trustworthy AI assurance.
notes:
## Key Design Decisions (from planning session)

### Architecture: Progressive Formalisation
- **Layer 1**: Formal vocabulary / lightweight ontology (JSON-LD @context)
- **Layer 2**: JSON-LD serialisation of existing data (backward compatible)
- **Layer 3**: MCP server with in-memory graph (high-level tools, not raw SPARQL)
- **Layer 4 (future)**: Graph database or triple store if query complexity/scale demands it

### Why JSON-LD over pure RDF or custom property graph
- JSON-LD is still JSON — existing code works unchanged
- Machine-readable semantics for any RDF-aware tool
- Future-proof for external ontology linking (NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001) — just add @context mappings
- Trivial conversion to RDF triples when needed
- Avoids heavyweight SPARQL tooling (LLMs won't generate SPARQL well anyway)

### Three target consumers
1. **LLM agent**: Helps users find techniques via natural language. Multi-dimensional filtering, relationship traversal.
2. **TEA Assurance Platform**: Users building structured assurance cases need techniques that produce evidence to support property claims. Graph path: GoalClaim → PropertyClaim → Evidence → Technique.
3. **Researchers**: Study patterns in Trustworthy AI assurance. Queryable, structured data.

### TEA Platform integration design input (from user)
The encoding of techniques in the platform should follow:
```
Goal Claim (e.g. 'System is fair')
  └── Property Claim (e.g. 'Equal treatment across protected groups')
        └── Evidence (e.g. 'Statistical parity metric = 0.95')
              └── Technique that produced the evidence
```
Evidence elements should have a new attribute/tag that links them to a specific technique. This mapping (claim→evidence→technique) is on the TEA Platform roadmap and tracked in beads.

### External ontology linking
- Between nice-to-have and core requirement
- Working with regulators and standards bodies already
- Architecture should not preclude this — JSON-LD approach supports it
- No immediate use case but clear future value

### Typed relationships (related_techniques)
- Currently untyped (just slug references)
- Will eventually add types: isAlternativeTo, buildsUpon, complementaryTo
- Not for initial implementation — vocabulary should include extension points

### MCP server deployment
- Bundled in the tea-techniques repo (not a separate service)
- Runs locally as an MCP server for LLM access
- Static JSON-LD files served from site for non-MCP consumers

### Cross-epic relationship
- Data quality loop (tea-techniques-rlw) improves cross-references and tag coverage
- Knowledge graph benefits from improved data but has NO hard dependency
- Both epics progress independently

### Dataset scale context
- 92 techniques, 7 goals, ~300 tags (4-level hierarchy), ~460 resources
- ~2,500 relationship edges total
- Comfortably fits in memory — no need for graph DB infrastructure initially
close_reason: All 6 sub-tasks complete: vocabulary, JSON-LD transformation, MCP tool design, MCP server, static endpoints, testing & docs

title: Design knowledge graph vocabulary
description: Define the entity types (Technique, AssuranceGoal, Tag, TagCategory, Resource), relationship types (addressesGoal, hasTag, relatedTo, citesResource, childOf), and tag hierarchy semantics. Document the vocabulary and create a JSON-LD @context. Consider future extension points: typed relationships (isAlternativeTo, buildsUpon, complementaryTo), external ontology alignment (NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001), and claim→evidence→technique mapping for TEA Platform integration. Output: vocabulary specification + JSON-LD @context file.
notes:
## Vocabulary Design Inputs

### TEA Platform claim→evidence→technique mapping
The vocabulary must model the path: GoalClaim → PropertyClaim → Evidence → Technique. Evidence elements need a new attribute/tag linking them to specific techniques. This mapping is on the TEA Platform roadmap — the vocabulary should define the relationship types even if the platform isn't ready to consume them yet.

### Future relationship types to plan for
- isAlternativeTo, buildsUpon, complementaryTo (typed related_techniques)
- producesEvidence, supportsClaimType (for TEA Platform)
- alignsWith (for external ontology links: NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001)

### Current entity-relationship summary
- Technique → AssuranceGoal: M:N via assurance_goals array (1 technique → 2-3 goals avg)
- Technique → Tag: M:N via tags array (~18 tags per technique avg)
- Technique → Technique: M:N via related_techniques slugs (3-4 avg, currently untyped)
- Technique → Resource: M:N via citation keys (2-10 avg)
- Tag hierarchy: 4 levels deep, 10 top-level categories
- Resources: 458 Zotero items with BetterBibTeX citation keys
close_reason: Created JSON-LD context at public/data/ld/context.jsonld, validation schema at schemas/jsonld-technique.schema.json, fixed TypeScript types in lib/types.ts to match source JSON field names

title: Implement JSON-LD data transformation
description: Build-time script (in scripts/) that transforms techniques.json into JSON-LD format with the vocabulary's @context. Must be backward-compatible — existing code that reads the JSON should still work. Integrate into the generate-data pipeline. Validate the JSON-LD output. Consider: should the source JSON be enriched in-place or should JSON-LD be a separate generated output?
close_reason: JSON-LD transformation script created and integrated into build pipeline. Tool interface designed (implemented in c17.4).

title: Design MCP server tool interface
description: Design the MCP tools the server will expose. Target three use cases: (a) LLM agent finding techniques via natural language queries (e.g. find_techniques, get_technique, compare_techniques, find_related), (b) TEA Platform integration for structured claim→evidence→technique queries (e.g. suggest_techniques_for_claim, find_evidence_types), (c) research queries and pattern analysis (e.g. explore_taxonomy, coverage_statistics). Define tool names, parameters, response formats. This is API design, not implementation.
close_reason: JSON-LD transformation script created and integrated into build pipeline. Tool interface designed (implemented in c17.4).

title: Implement MCP server with in-memory graph
description: Build an MCP server (bundled in the tea-techniques repo) that loads JSON-LD data at startup, builds an in-memory graph index (adjacency lists, tag hierarchy tree, inverted indexes for search), and exposes the designed MCP tools. The server should handle: multi-dimensional filtering, relationship traversal, tag hierarchy navigation, technique comparison, and coverage analysis. Test with Claude to verify tool usability.
close_reason: MCP server implemented with all 10 tools, entry point, data loader with caching, in-memory knowledge graph. Static JSON-LD endpoints generated by generate-jsonld.js. Both type-checks pass.

title: Expose static JSON-LD API endpoints
description: Serve the JSON-LD enriched data as static files from the site (via /public/data/). This provides a machine-readable API for non-MCP consumers (TEA Platform, researchers, web crawlers). Consider: individual technique endpoints, collection endpoints, vocabulary/context endpoint, content negotiation documentation. Include usage documentation.
close_reason: MCP server implemented with all 10 tools, entry point, data loader with caching, in-memory knowledge graph. Static JSON-LD endpoints generated by generate-jsonld.js. Both type-checks pass.

title: Integration testing and documentation
description: End-to-end validation: MCP server answers representative queries correctly across all three use cases (LLM agent, TEA Platform, research). Static endpoints serve valid JSON-LD. Vocabulary is documented for external consumers. Write usage documentation for configuring the MCP server with Claude (claude_desktop_config.json setup). Include example queries and expected responses.
close_reason: 51 vitest tests (indexes, queries, integration), 2 MDX doc pages (overview + MCP server guide), all checks pass

title: getRelatedTechniques ignores related_techniques data, returns random techniques
Add academic literature MCP servers
description:
Create MCP server integrations for academic literature APIs to support the enhancement agent's exploration activities:

1. **arXiv MCP**
   - Search papers by topic/keyword
   - Fetch paper metadata (authors, abstract, date, categories)
   - Check for paper revisions/updates
   - Rate limiting and caching

2. **Semantic Scholar MCP**
   - Search papers with citation context
   - Get citation graphs (what cites this, what does this cite)
   - Influence/impact metrics
   - Author information

3. **DOI Resolution**
   - Validate DOI links
   - Fetch metadata from DOI
   - Detect broken/moved resources

These MCPs enable:
- Resource freshness checking (are there newer versions?)
- Literature coverage analysis (who's citing this technique?)
- Relationship discovery (papers that cite multiple techniques)

Initially can use WebFetch with APIs; dedicated MCPs improve reliability and caching.

Reference: docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md (Open Questions section)

title: Add freshness and coverage metrics to quality report
description:
Extend the quality report with metrics that have no ceiling, ensuring the improvement loop never considers itself 'complete':

1. **Resource Freshness Decay**
   ```json
   {
     "resource_freshness": {
       "avg_age_years": 3.2,
       "oldest_resource_years": 8,
       "resources_older_than_5y": 12,
       "freshness_score": 0.72
     }
   }
   ```
   - Calculate age of each cited resource
   - Freshness score decays over time (never 100%)
   - Flag resources older than threshold

2. **Literature Coverage Drift**
   ```json
   {
     "literature_coverage": {
       "technique_citations_found": 45,
       "citations_in_zotero": 12,
       "coverage_ratio": 0.27,
       "new_papers_this_quarter": 8
     }
   }
   ```
   - Track how many papers cite/reference each technique
   - Compare to what's in Zotero library
   - Surface when literature outpaces our coverage

3. **Peer Comparison** (relative metrics)
   - How does each technique compare to similar techniques?
   - Relative completeness, not absolute

Update:
- scripts/quality/quality-rules.js
- scripts/quality/generate-quality-report.js
- schemas/quality-report.schema.json

Reference: docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md (Metrics Evolution section)

title: Create fleshed-out cardiac DT assurance case JSON
description: Build a proper assurance case for the cardiac digital twin (PAH severity assessment). Starting from the illustrative diagram (G1→P1/P2/P3→E1/E2/E3), flesh it out with: context nodes, strategies for decomposing G1 (e.g. argument over fidelity, accuracy, interpretability), assumptions, justifications, and properly structured property claims and evidence. Output as a valid AssurancePlatform v1.0 nested-tree JSON file.
close_reason: Assurance case JSON created and successfully imported into the TEA Platform. 16 elements, 3 evidence links, 4 strategies, 8 property claims (5 with gaps), 3 evidence nodes.

title: Improve MCP server installation and distribution
description:
Current MCP server install instructions require cloning the repo first, which is poor UX. Plan and implement one of these options:

1. Publish the MCP server to npm so it can be run directly with npx/bunx (preferred)
2. Improve documentation to clearly explain the repo clone requirement

This needs proper planning to determine the best distribution approach.

title: Self-Improving Data Quality Loop
description: A system for identifying and improving data quality issues in the TEA techniques dataset. Two components: (1) a programmatic quality report script that analyses tag coverage, summary statistics, cross-references, and completeness scoring, producing a structured JSON report; (2) an AI-powered Claude agent that consumes the report and proposes improvements via PRs.
notes:
## Key Design Decisions (from initial planning session)

### Scope clarifications
- **Ratings (complexity/computational cost):** Bumped/commented out. Defining a principled metric is OUT OF SCOPE for this epic.
- **Acronyms:** Optional field, not a quality concern. Only some techniques have them.
- **Cross-reference validation:** Important prerequisite for the knowledge graph epic (idea #2, not yet created).
- **Resource curation via Zotero MCP:** Out of scope for the AI agent. Zotero handles metadata/citation accuracy separately.
- **Suggesting new techniques:** Out of scope. Reports reveal imbalances but don't propose new techniques.

### Architecture decisions
- **Single script, single JSON report, two consumers:** One script with four analysis modules (tag coverage, summary stats, cross-references, completeness scoring) produces one structured JSON report. Consumed by (1) GitHub Actions cron fortnightly and (2) the AI agent.
- **Tag gap detection is split between script and agent:** Script identifies WHICH techniques are missing tags from WHICH categories (using universal rules, goal-conditional rules, and statistical outlier detection). Agent proposes WHICH SPECIFIC tag values to add.
- **Three levels of programmatic tag gap detection:**
  1. Universal category rules — categories every technique should have (e.g. lifecycle-stage, applicable-models)
  2. Goal-conditional rules — e.g. Explainability → should have explanatory-scope tag
  3. Statistical outlier detection — flag techniques missing tags that 90%+ of peers have

### Painful quality issues (user-identified)
- Tag coverage and completeness is the #1 pain point — wizard doesn't work well because tags are incomplete
- Technique coverage across assurance goals is imbalanced (e.g. 44 explainability, 11 privacy)
- Tag taxonomy may have gaps — e.g. explainability subcategories may need additional values

---

## Design Evolution (rlw.4 completion, 2026-02-05)

**Reference:** docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md

### Major changes from initial plan

1. **Local execution, not PR workflow**
   - Original: Agent creates branch, opens PR for review
   - New: Local Claude Code execution via `/enhance-dataset` slash command
   - Human reviews proposals locally, approves/rejects, commits when ready
   - Can be cron-scheduled: `claude -p "/enhance-dataset" --agent enhancement-orchestrator`

2. **Multi-agent consensus approach**
   - Three specialist perspectives: Academic Researcher, Industry Practitioner, Standards Body
   - 2/3 quorum required for proposals to proceed
   - Each specialist runs as parallel sub-agent with access to Zotero, web search, TEA MCP

3. **Never-complete metrics**
   - Quality report metrics must avoid hard ceilings
   - New metrics: resource freshness decay, literature coverage drift
   - Ensures improvement loop always finds opportunities

4. **Taxonomy governance**
   - Agent can propose new tag values with evidence
   - Human approval required for taxonomy changes
   - No community RFC process yet (deferred)

### Updated issue structure
- rlw.5: Implement three specialist perspective skills
- rlw.6: Implement orchestrator agent + /enhance-dataset command
- kjb: Add freshness/coverage metrics to quality report
- hr2: Add academic literature MCPs (arXiv, Semantic Scholar) - P3, deferred
close_reason: All core deliverables complete (rlw.1-7 closed). Remaining children (kjb, hr2) are deferred follow-on improvements that stand independently.

title: Design quality scoring methodology and report schema
description: Define the JSON schema for the quality report output. Design the scoring methodology: which tag categories are universal (required for all techniques), which are goal-conditional (required based on assurance goals), and thresholds for statistical outlier detection. Define the completeness scoring formula.
notes:
## Design considerations
- Universal tag categories (apply to all techniques): needs definition, likely includes lifecycle-stage, applicable-models, technique-type, data-type
- Goal-conditional rules: e.g. Explainability → explanatory-scope, Fairness → fairness-approach
- Statistical outlier threshold: suggested 90%+ peer coverage as flag threshold
- Completeness score formula: weighted combination of tag category coverage, description length, use case count, limitation count, resource count, related techniques presence
- Report schema should include: generated_at timestamp, summary section, tag_coverage section (universal/goal-conditional/statistical), cross_references section (invalid/asymmetric/orphans), completeness_scores array (ranked worst-first)
- Tag taxonomy gaps to consider: explainability has subcategories like Attribution Method and Explainability Target that may need additional values
close_reason: Implemented quality scoring: rules config, JSON schema, report generator with weighted completeness scoring (avg 81.2/100). Should-rules at 0.5 weight, goal_tag_depth at 20%.

title: Implement quality report script
description: Single script in scripts/quality/ that produces a structured JSON report with four analysis sections: (a) tag coverage — universal gaps, goal-conditional gaps, statistical outliers; (b) dataset summary statistics — technique counts by goal, tags per technique, resource counts; (c) cross-reference validation — invalid slugs, asymmetric relationships, orphan techniques; (d) completeness scoring — per-technique score ranked worst-first. Output as JSON to reports/ or stdout.
close_reason: Already implemented as part of rlw.1: scripts/quality/generate-quality-report.js covers all four analysis modules (tag coverage, summary stats, cross-reference validation, completeness scoring).

title: Set up GitHub Actions cron workflow
description: GitHub Actions workflow that runs the quality report fortnightly. Commits the report JSON to the repo (or posts a summary to an issue). Provides visibility into data health trends over time.
close_reason: Closed

title: Design AI agent improvement methodology
description: Define the methodology for the AI improvement agent: how it reads and prioritises from the quality report, how it reasons about tag additions (using existing taxonomy vs proposing new values), how it improves related technique cross-references, and how it scopes work (single technique, category, assurance goal, or worst-N). Define the skill structure and agent configuration.
close_reason: Design document completed at docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md. Defines multi-agent consensus approach with academic/practitioner/standards perspectives, 2/3 quorum, local Claude Code execution via slash command.

title: Implement specialist perspective skills
description:
Create three specialist skill files that encode domain expertise for the multi-agent consensus approach:

1. **academic-perspective.md** - Academic researcher perspective
   - Literature search via Zotero MCP and web
   - Citation pattern analysis
   - Methodological rigour assessment
   - Resource validity checking

2. **practitioner-perspective.md** - Industry practitioner perspective
   - Tooling/library availability (PyPI, GitHub)
   - Real-world applicability assessment
   - Deployment considerations
   - Practical complexity evaluation

3. **standards-perspective.md** - Standards body perspective
   - Regulatory framework alignment (ISO, NIST, EU AI Act)
   - Audit requirement mapping
   - Documentation standards
   - Governance considerations

Each skill should:
- Accept technique data and quality issues as input
- Return structured analysis with proposals and confidence scores
- Be invocable as parallel sub-agents by the orchestrator

Reference: docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md
close_reason: Created three specialist perspective skills in .claude/skills/enhancement/: academic-perspective (282 lines), practitioner-perspective (332 lines), standards-perspective (367 lines). Skills are orchestrator-invoked with structured output format for multi-agent consensus. Files remain local/untracked per project convention for .claude/ directory.

title: Implement orchestrator agent and slash command
description:
Create the enhancement orchestrator agent and invocation mechanism:

1. **enhance-dataset.md** agent file
   - Loads quality report from reports/quality-report.json
   - Parses scope from command args (--technique, --goal, --worst N)
   - Builds prioritised work queue based on summary stats
   - Spawns specialist agents in parallel (Task tool)
   - Implements 2/3 consensus logic
   - Generates structured proposals with evidence
   - Applies approved changes to techniques.json
   - Logs decisions to .enhancement-log.json (gitignored)

2. **/enhance-dataset** slash command
   - Entry point for local invocation
   - Supports flags: --technique <slug>, --goal <goal>, --worst <N>, --batch-size <N>
   - Default behaviour: prioritise by issue density and score

3. **Proposal output format**
   - Change type, specific proposal, evidence from each perspective
   - Consensus indicators (agree/disagree/uncertain per agent)
   - Confidence level (high/medium/low)

4. **Enhancement log** (.enhancement-log.json)
   - Track rejections with reasons and permanence flag
   - Track approvals for audit trail
   - Gitignored but checked before proposing

Invocation: `claude -p "/enhance-dataset"` or cron-scheduled

Reference: docs/design/rlw-4-agent-improvement-methodology.md
close_reason: Implemented orchestrator agent and slash command. Files created: .claude/agents/enhance-dataset.md (orchestrator), .claude/commands/enhance-dataset.md (entry point), .enhancement-log.json (gitignored log)

title: End-to-end integration test
description: Run the full loop: generate quality report → agent reads report → agent improves techniques → PR opened. Verify the PR contains sensible changes, the report accurately identified the gaps, and the workflow is repeatable.
close_reason: Integration testing complete: 3 techniques enhanced (internal-review-boards, red-teaming, human-in-the-loop-safeguards), 4 bugs/improvements identified

title: Enhancement skills should do comprehensive tag audits, not just address quality gaps
description:
## Issue

During rlw.7 integration testing, we found that the enhancement specialist skills focus narrowly on addressing **quality report gaps** rather than doing **comprehensive tag audits**.

## Example

For `internal-review-boards`:
- Quality report flagged: missing depth-3 assurance-goal-category tags
- Skills proposed: added those specific depth-3 tags ✓
- Skills missed: IRBs can be used at multiple lifecycle stages not currently tagged

Current lifecycle tags:
- `lifecycle-stage/project-planning`
- `lifecycle-stage/model-development`
- `lifecycle-stage/system-deployment-and-use`
- `lifecycle-stage/post-deployment` (added)

Missing lifecycle stages where IRBs could apply:
- `problem-formulation` - ethical review of problem framing
- `data-collection` - review of data sourcing ethics
- `model-evaluation` - review of evaluation criteria

## Proposed Changes

Update specialist skills to:

1. **Review all tag categories** for completeness, not just flagged gaps
2. **Cross-reference with domain knowledge** (e.g., Standards perspective knows NIST AI RMF lifecycle)
3. **Propose comprehensive tag sets** rather than incremental additions

## Files to Modify

- `.claude/skills/enhancement/academic-perspective/SKILL.md`
- `.claude/skills/enhancement/practitioner-perspective/SKILL.md`
- `.claude/skills/enhancement/standards-perspective/SKILL.md`

## Discovered

During rlw.7 integration testing of the enhancement system.

title: Use TEA Techniques MCP to recommend techniques for gaps
description: For each identified gap (property claim without sufficient evidence), use the TEA Techniques MCP suggest_techniques_for_claim tool with appropriate context (model type, data type, lifecycle stage) to find recommended techniques. Review and rank the suggestions.
close_reason: Completed MCP evaluation across 5 gap claims. Found critical bug in suggest_techniques_for_claim (AND-all-terms search), no model-type filtering, keyword ambiguity, and process techniques buried by ML explainability. Detailed scoring in new findings issue.

title: CI/CD pipeline blocked by Biome lint errors on generated files
description:
## Bug

The last 7 CI/CD pipeline runs on alan-turing-institute/tea-techniques have failed, blocking deployment to GitHub Pages since Feb 16. The Deploy to GitHub Pages job depends on Quality Gates, which fails at the `pnpm lint` (Biome) step.

## Root Cause

Two generated files fail Biome formatting checks:

1. **reports/quality-report.json** — Auto-committed by the quality-report.yml workflow on Feb 15 with `[skip ci]`, so Biome never checked its formatting. The `reports/` directory was not excluded from Biome.

2. **public/data/ld/graph.jsonld** — Modified on Feb 15 by `feat(claims): integrate claims into pipeline`. The Biome config excluded `!public/data/**/*.json` but the `.jsonld` extension is not matched by `*.json`, so this file was checked.

## Fix

Added two exclusion patterns to `biome.jsonc` `files.includes`:
- `!reports/**/*.json` — exclude auto-generated quality reports
- `!public/data/**/*.jsonld` — exclude generated JSON-LD files

## Impact

- Last successful deployment: Feb 14
- Live documentation site is stale since Feb 16
close_reason: Added Biome exclusions for reports/**/*.json and public/data/**/*.jsonld in biome.jsonc
